paper_title: Safe Few-Step Generation via Velocity Editing

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Flow matching has recently emerged as a strong paradigm for state-of-the-art text-to-image (T2I) generation, enabling high-quality generation with a small number of sampling steps. As these models are increasingly integrated into real-world applications, ensuring safe and non-sensitive content generation has become a critical requirement. However, adapting safety and concept removal methods to this new generation framework remains an open challenge. Specifically, prior methods largely rely on iterative trajectory steering across a number of denoising steps or on CLIP-centric prompt embedding manipulation. These design assumptions pose fundamental bottlenecks for safety in flow matching-based T2I generation, where limited sampling steps constrain iterative correction and modern context-aware text encoders diminish the effectiveness of embedding-level interventions. In this paper, we propose VESFlow, a training-free safety method tailored to flow matching with extremely few sampling steps. Leveraging the fact that flow matching models learn the marginal velocity, we directly edit the velocity field via a safe-conditional posterior. VESFlow steers the trajectory toward safe outputs while leaving the conditioning prompt unchanged. Building on the observation that VESFlow leaves outputs unchanged under benign prompts, we further introduce a risk score-based filtering that bypasses velocity editing to reduce computational cost while preserving benign prompt generation. Based on this filtering, we propose VESFlow+, a stronger variant of VESFlow that not only edits the velocity toward the safe direction, but also pushes it away from the unsafe direction. Experimental results show that VESFlow+ removes the target concept, reducing the attack success rate by NudeNet to 6.3% on Ring-A-Bell and 6.8% on MMA-Diffusion on the 4-step MeanFlow model, while preserving fidelity on benign prompts.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Safe Few-Step Generation via Velocity Editing Yujin Choi1 2 1NTU Singapore Jaehong Yoon1 2UNIST 6 2 0 2 J 2 2 ] . [ 1 7 6 2 3 2 . 6 0 6 2 : r {cs-yujin.choi, jaehong.yoon}@ntu.edu.sg Project Page: https://uzn36.github.io/VESFlow

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Flow matching has recently emerged as strong paradigm for state-of-the-art textto-image (T2I) generation, enabling high-quality generation with small number of sampling steps. As these models are increasingly integrated into real-world applications, ensuring safe and non-sensitive content generation has become critical requirement. However, adapting safety and concept removal methods to this new generation framework remains an open challenge. Specifically, prior methods largely rely on iterative trajectory steering across number of denoising steps or on CLIP-centric prompt embedding manipulation. These design assumptions pose fundamental bottlenecks for safety in flow matching-based T2I generation, where limited sampling steps constrain iterative correction and modern context-aware text encoders diminish the effectiveness of embedding-level interventions. In this paper, we propose VESFLOW, training-free safety method tailored to flow matching with extremely few sampling steps. Leveraging the fact that flow matching models learn the marginal velocity (or average velocity in MeanFlow), we directly edit the velocity field via Bayesian decomposition of the safe-conditional posterior. VESFLOW steers the trajectory toward safe outputs while leaving the conditioning prompt unchanged. Building on the observation that VESFLOW leaves outputs unchanged under benign prompts, we further introduce risk score-based filtering that bypasses velocity editing to reduce computational cost while preserving benign prompt generation. Based on this filtering, we propose VESFLOW+, stronger variant of VESFLOWthat not only edits the velocity toward the safe direction, but also pushes it away from the unsafe direction, once prompt is classified as unsafe. Experimental results show that our method removes the target concept, reducing the attack success rate by NudeNet to 6.3% on Ring-A-Bell and 6.8% on MMA-Diffusion on the 4-step MeanFlow model, while preserving fidelity on benign prompts. Warning: this paper contains content that may be inappropriate or offensive, including censored images of nudity and sexually explicit text prompts

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The iterative sampling process of diffusion models [4, 25, 8] enables high-quality generation, but its substantial computational cost limits practical deployment [27]. This bottleneck has driven growing interest in few-step generative models. Flow matching [16] addresses this challenge by learning velocity field rather than noise-prediction, yielding near-linear sampling trajectories. Due to these advantages, flow matching-based models are now widely adopted for developing high-performance text-to-image (T2I) generative models, including stable diffusion (SD) v3 [5] and FLUX [1]. Building on this linear velocity formulation, MeanFlow [7] further reduces the number of sampling steps by Corresponding Author Preprint. learning the average velocity between two time steps, rather than instantaneous velocity as in the standard flow matching models. This enables extremely few-step generation, including one-step generation on ImageNet [3]. As few-step generative models play an expanding role in real-world applications, ensuring their safety is increasingly critical. However, while training-free safeguard methods offer practical flexibility across diverse generative frameworks, most of them largely rely on the iterative guidance during the sampling process, making effective deployment in the few-step generation regime challenging: one straightforward approach is leveraging classifier-free guidance (CFG) [8], by replacing the unconditional score with score conditioned on an unsafe negative prompt [23]. More recently, line of studies introduces negative guidance [10, 18, 12, 11] based on the predicted clean sample at each time step t: when the predicted sample x0 approaches an unsafe subspace, repulsive guidance term is injected to push the trajectory away. Although effective in many-step samplers, such trajectory-level interventions rely on the cumulative effect of small per-step corrections, making them inherently sensitive to the number of sampling steps. In the few-step regime, the extremely limited correction horizon forces per-step guidance to be either too weak to reliably suppress unsafe content or too strong, compromising image fidelity and benign prompt alignment. direct alternative to circumvent the step-count limitation is to edit the prompt embedding [28]. However, the effectiveness of these methods is heavily constrained by the underlying text encoder. Specifically, modern State-of-The-Art (SoTA) T2I models [1, 5] employ language model-based encoders [22], which represent prompts through more sentence-level, context-dependent embeddings. As result, toxic concepts become substantially harder to precisely localize or remove through token-level prompt embedding manipulation alone [6]. In this paper, we propose velocity editing for safe flow matching (VESFLOW), training-free safety method designed for the few-step regime of flow-matching-based generative models. Our key idea is to replace trajectory-level correction with velocity-level editing. Motivated by the fact that flow matching models learn the marginal velocity vector, we directly edit the velocity field toward safe-conditional posterior without modifying the text embedding. Specifically, given pre-trained velocity field v(xtc) conditioned on prompt embedding c, we derive the modified vector field v(xtc) = v(xtc, = 1), where = 1 denotes the event that the final clean sample x0 belongs to the safe region S. Furthermore, we introduce risk score filtering, which allows not only to reduce the computational cost of our method, but also to preserve fidelity for the benign prompt. Based on this filtering, we propose VESFLOW+, stronger variant that provides enhanced safety protection. On the 4-step MeanFlow model, VESFLOW reduces the NudeNet attack success rate to 15.2% on Ring-A-Bell and 7.5% on MMA-Diffusion , while VESFLOW+ further reduces these rate to 6.3% and 6.8%, respectively. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: We proposed VESFLOW, training-free safety method for few-step flow-matching-based generative models that directly edits the velocity field toward safe-conditional posterior. With risk score filtering, we reduce per-step gradient computation and improve fidelity on benign prompts. Based on this mechanism, we further proposed VESFLOW+, stronger variant that attracts the trajectory toward safe regions and repels it from unsafe regions. Experimental results support that VESFLOW efficiently suppresses toxic concept, while preserving the benign generation performance. Ablation studies further confirm the robustness of our method to the choice of scorer and evaluator.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: Training-free concept removal. Recent training-free safety methods suppress unsafe generation by modifying the sampling trajectory at inference time, without updating model parameters. common strategy is to modify the sampling process using safety-related guidance. Safe Denoiser [10] directly modifies the sampling trajectory using negation set and derives safe denoiser that steers samples away from regions to be avoided. Safety-Guided Flow (SGF) [11] further provides unified view of negative guidance by recasting Safe Denoiser and related repulsive sampling methods under maximum mean discrepancy (MMD)-potential formulation, and identifies critical time window in which safety guidance should be active. Shielded Diffusion [12] is also related in its use of 2 trajectory-level repellency, although its primary focus is protected-reference avoidance and diversity rather than toxic concept suppression. Rather than modifying the latent trajectory, several studies modified the conditioning text embedding for pre-trained diffusion process. Building on classifier-free guidance (CFG) [9], Safe Latent Diffusion (SLD) [23] replaces the unconditional score with one conditioned on an unsafe negative prompt to suppress harmful generation. SAFREE [30] constructs toxic-concept subspace in the text-embedding space and projects prompt token embeddings away from it. Semantic Surgery [28] further performs calibrated vector subtraction on text embeddings prior to sampling, enabling concept erasure without explicit negative prompts. Recently, Safe Text embedding Guidance (STG) [18] dynamically adjusts the prompt embedding during sampling using safety function evaluated on the expected denoised image. Concept removal in flow matching As flow matching has become central to SoTA T2I generation, concept removal methods for flow-matching-based models have begun to emerge. EraseAnything [6] formulates erasure as bi-level optimization problem with LoRA-based parameter tuning for rectified flow transformers, while EraseFlow [13] casts concept unlearning as trajectory-balancebased exploration over denoising paths via GFlowNets. Training-free methods such as SGF [11] can also be formulated for flow models, but they still operate by perturbing the sampling trajectory. Our work instead focuses on the few-step regime, where such trajectory-level corrections may be insufficient, and formulates safety directly at the velocity-field level.

sections.title: 3 Background and Motivation
sections.content: 3.1 Flow Matching Models Flow matching [16] learns velocity field that transports samples from simple prior to the data distribution, has been adopted in recent SoTA T2I models, including SD v3 [5] and FLUX [1]. Following common practice, we consider linear path between Gaussian noise x1 and the data sample x0: xt = (1 t) x0 + tx1, x1 (0, I). (1) For given pair (x0, x1), the conditional velocity along this path is x1 x0, and the marginal velocity field is thereby given by vt(xt) = E[x1 x0 xt]. (2) Sampling is then performed by an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) defined by continuous-time velocity field vt: vt(xt) = 1 1 xt 1 xt log pt(xt). (3) While flow matching reduces the number of function evaluations compared to diffusion models, the demand for further reduction to extremely few-step, or even one-step generation continues to grow. MeanFlow models [7] enable few-step generation by modeling the average velocity u(vt, r, tc) = (cid:82) 1 vτ (xτ c)dτ between two time steps, rather than the instantaneous velocity. They achieve tr high-fidelity ImageNet generation performance even in one-step generations. Building on this, Pu et al. [20] distill the flow matching model (FLUX) into MeanFlow, achieving high-fidelity T2I generation in 4 steps. 3.2 Velocity Editing for Few-Step Generative Flow Matching Models Existing training-free methods remove toxic concepts by injecting small guidance term at each denoising step of an ODE solver (e.g., Euler sampler), so that the cumulative effect across many steps gradually steers the trajectory toward the safe region while maintaining sample fidelity [10, 11, 12]. However, in few-step generation, this strategy becomes unreliable: the small number of sampling steps provide insufficient opportunity for trajectory correction. As result, existing methods struggle to simultaneously suppress toxic content while preserving benign prompt fidelity. Whereas prior methods could restrict guidance to an early stage of sampling to preserve fidelity, few-step generation forces guidance to be applied at nearly every step. Moreover, the few-step regime does not allow guidance to accumulate gradually, often requiring larger per-step guidance. However, naively increasing the per-step scale tends to push samples off the data manifold, degrading fidelity rather than improving safety. Fig. 1 illustrates these failures modes on 1D toy example. Let v(xtc) denote the conditional velocity vector field constructed by [1, 1], where = +1 corresponds to fully safe prompt and = 1 to fully unsafe prompt. We visualize the velocity field as the background under the unsafe condition (c = 1). Trajectory-level guidance methods leave v(xtc) unchanged and instead inject stepwise guidance. With large number of sampling steps (Fig. 1 Left), most trajectories reach the safe region; however, with only few sampling steps (Fig. 1 Right), the cumulative effect of this per-step guidance becomes insufficient to redirect the trajectory, and samples may converge to unsafe regions. Detailed setup of this example is provided in Sec. A. Figure 1: Sampling trajectories under trajectory-level guidance across different sampling steps. Blue and red denote safe and unsafe generated samples x0, respectively. Another major approach is embedding editing, which modifies the prompt embedding itself prior to sampling, i.e., globally transforms the field from v(xtc) to v(xtc) via modified embedding c. Representatively, SAFREE [30] projects the prompt embedding away from toxic concept subspace, whereas Semantic Surgery [28] performs vector subtraction to remove toxic concepts while preserving neutral concepts. Although these methods may be relatively robust to few-step guidance limitations, they often face compatibility challenges with modern text encoders. As discussed in [6], earlier T2I models (e.g., SD v1.4) rely on CLIP-based text encoders, which are trained via imagetext contrastive matching and produce token-level embeddings. In contrast, recent flow-matching-based T2I models, such as FLUX[1] and SD v3[5] adopt large language model encoders (e.g., T5) trained on long-context corpora to capture richer semantics. This sentence-level encoding makes toxic concepts substantially harder to isolate and remove [6], as harmful semantics are distributed across the full embedding, rather than localized to individual tokens. Detailed explanation of these challenges are provided in Sec. B.

sections.title: 4 VESFlow: Velocity Editing for Safe Flow Matching
sections.content: Motivated by the analysis in the previous section, we directly edit the velocity field of the pretrained model, without modifying the prompt embedding or relying on accumulated trajectorylevel corrections. To this end, we focus on how flow matching learns the marginal velocity, v(xtc) = Ex0,x1p(x0,x1xt,c)[x1 x0]. (4) For the data space , we partition it into safe subset and its complement S, and introduce binary safety indicator {0, 1} where = 1 denotes the event x0 S, following [18]. Then, we define the safe-conditional velocity as v(xtc) = v(xtc, = 1) = Ex0,x1p(x0,x1xt,c),x0S [x1 x0], which is the conditional expectation of x1 x0 restricted to trajectories whose endpoint lies in the safe region. Fig. 2 visualize the resulting velocity vector v(xtc) under the same pre-trained model as Fig. 1 and unsafe condition (c = 1). This safe-conditional velocity steers trajectories toward the safe region regardless the number of sampling steps. Detailed setups for this motivating example are in Sec. Figure 2: Sampling trajectories under the safe-conditional velocity field across different sampling steps. Blue and red denote safe and unsafe generated samples x0, respectively. Problem formulation We consider pre-trained flow matching T2I model and aim to suppress the generation of harmful content without retraining. Given prompt embedding C, our goal is (i) to preserve original generation fidelity for benign c, and (ii) to steer unsafe generation toward safe output. Given pretrained conditional velocity field vt(xtc), our objective is to construct safe-conditional velocity field vt(xtc) := vt(xtc, = 1), which steers the sampling dynamics toward safe outputs. 4 4.1 VESFLOW: Velocity Editing for Safe Flow Matching We derive the velocity update required to transform the original conditional velocity field vt(xtc) into the safe-conditional velocity field vt(xtc) = vt(xtc, = 1). Using the probability-flow form of the flow-matching velocity in eq. (3), we obtain vt(xtc) vt(xtc) = vt(xtc, = 1) vt(xtc) = 1 1 = xt log xt log pt(xtc, = 1) + (cid:19) (cid:18) pt(xtc) pt(xtc, = 1) xt log pt(xtc) 1 . (5) Then, by Bayess rule, we have: (cid:18) (cid:19) xt log pt(xtc) pt(xtc, = 1) = xt log (cid:18) p(s = 1c) (cid:19) pt(s = 1xt, c) = xt log pt(s = 1xt, c). (6) Following [18], we introduce pre-trained safety verifier (e.g., nudity detector). By using first-order Taylor approximation on the expected clean data we approximate the probability of being unsafe: Ex0p(x0xt,c)[x0] = xt vt(xtc) := x, pt(s = 0xt, c) = Ex0p(x0xt,c)p(s = 0x0) = Ex0p(x0xt,c)[g(x0)] g(Ex0p(x0xt,c)[x0]) = g(x). Therefore, we can get: xt log pt(s = 1xt, c) log(1 g(x)) = xg(x) 1 g(x) . This yields our safety score guidance: vt(xtc) vt(xtc) = 1 (cid:26) xg(x) 1 g(x) (cid:27) := v. (7) (8) (9) (10) This guidance has two intuitive properties. First, the factor (1 g(x))1 increases the update magnitude when the predicted clean sample is likely to be unsafe, yielding stronger correction for high-risk generations. Second, the factor t/(1t) acts as natural time-dependent scheduler inherited from the flow-matching velocity parameterization. The guidance is stronger at early sampling times, where is close to 1, and gradually vanishes as 0. This behavior is consistent with prior observations that early-stage interventions are particularly important for safe generation [11]. To prevent t/(1 t) from diverging at 1, we set the upper bound of time variable tmax < 1, replacing the factor with t/(1 min(tmax, t)). scaling hyperparameter λ then controls the overall guidance strength: at each denoising step, the velocity is updated as + λ v. We refer to this velocity editing procedure via the safety score guidance as Velocity Editing for Safe Flow matching (VESFLOW). Extension to MeanFlow model Our method can be naturally extended to MeanFlow [7], which learns an average velocity rather than an instantaneous velocity and enables one-step generation on ImageNet [3]. For time interval [r, t], the average velocity is defined as u(xt, r, tc) = 1 (cid:90) r vτ (xτ c) dτ, u(xt, r, tc) = 1 (cid:90) vτ (xτ c) dτ, (11) where vτ (xτ c) = vτ (xτ c, = 1). Thus, u[r,t] := u(xt, r, tc) u(xt, r, tc) (cid:90) = 1 (vτ (xτ c) vτ (xτ c)) dτ. (12) 5 Safe prompt: smiling girl, Toxic prompt: smiling naked girl exposing her breast Figure 3: Effect of guidance scale on safe and toxic prompts. For safe prompts, the output remains nearly unchanged as the guidance scale increases. For toxic prompts, stronger guidance progressively suppresses unsafe content, with the usual trade-off between safety and fidelity. Since MeanFlow is designed to learn the average velocity along near-linear sampling trajectories, the velocity correction can be assumed to vary slowly within the interval [r, t]. We therefore approximate the integral using the endpoint: vt = 1 (cid:26) xg(x) 1 g(x) (cid:27) . (13) Therefore, the same safety score guidance derived for standard flow matching can be applied to MeanFlow models by editing the predicted average velocity. 4.2 Risk Score-Based Filtering VESFLOW preserves the original sampling trajectory when the baseline generation is already safe. Specifically, it minimally edits the velocity for confidently safe input prompts since smooth binary safety classifier produces vanishing gradients, i.e., for g[x] 0, the editing term log(1g(x)) 0, which ensures that the velocity edit satisfies vt vt 0, for any = 1. To verify this property, we apply VESFLOW while omitting the first sampling step. As shown in Fig. 3, increasing guidance scale leaves already safe generations unchanged while selectively modifying unsafe outputs. This property helps preserve the original generation behavior for benign prompts: even when VESFLOW is applied, the update becomes negligible when the predicted clean sample is confidently safe. However, VESFLOW still requires computing the score-guidance term, which involves the gradient of the scorer with respect to the predicted clean sample at every sampling step. Thus, even when the resulting update is near-zero, evaluating xg(x) incurs additional computational overhead. To avoid unnecessary computation, we introduce risk score-based filtering, which determines whether the prompt itself is unsafe, and applies VESFLOW only to unsafe prompts. We construct set of text encodings for fixed list of unsafe-concept words following SAFREE[30], and define the risk score for prompt as: e, r(c) = max eC where ec and are the ℓ2 normalized CLIP text embeddings [21] of the input prompt and the unsafe concept prompts, respectively. We apply VESFLOW only when r(c) > τ , with conservative threshold (τ = 0.3 in our experiments) to prevent unsafe prompts from being misclassified as safe. Since this filtering is performed only once at the prompt level and requires only single similarity computation, the additional cost is negligible while fully preserving the original generation quality on benign prompts. (14) This filtering enables stronger variant of VESFLOW, which we term VESFLOW+. The original formulation in eq. (5) uses v(xtc, = 1) v(xtc) as safety score guidance, where v(xtc) is the marginal velocity averaged over both safe and unsafe components. Once filtering identifies prompts with high p(s = 0c), we can replace the marginal term with the unsafe-conditional velocity, yielding the editing vector v(xtc, = 1) v(xtc, = 0). Whereas the original guidance steers the trajectory toward the safe-conditional velocity, the stronger variant simultaneously pulls the trajectory toward the safe-conditional velocity and pushes it away from the unsafe-conditional velocity. The full derivation of this is provided in Sec. C. 6 Table 1: Safety and quality comparison across few-step concept removal baselines and ours. sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Experimental Settings Setup We evaluate VESFLOW and VESFLOW+ on few-step flow-matching-based T2I models. We use FLUX.1-lite-8B [2] with 8 sampling steps, which provide sufficient generation quality in the few-step regime. We use the MeanFlow-distilled T2I model from [20] with 4 sampling steps, as recommended by the authors. Evaluation For safety evaluation, we use the Ring-A-Bell [26] (nudity and violence), and additionally MMA-Diffusion [29](nudity) benchmarks. We report Attack Success Rate (ASR) and Toxic Rate (TR), following [11]: ASR is the fraction of generated images whose predicted toxic class probability exceeds 0.6, and TR is the average toxic class probability across generated images. The toxic class probability are computed via NudeNet [19] for nudity and Q16 [24] for violence. To evaluate benign generation quality, we use prompts from MS-COCO [15] and report FID and CLIP score. Baselines We compare VESFLOW(+) with representative training-free safety methods: SGF [11], STG [18], SAFREE [30], and Semantic Surgery [28]. To avoid underestimating their safety performance in the few-step regime, we tune the sampling trajectory over the full sampling trajectory and the sub-interval used in their original many-step setups, and tune their guidance strengths accordingly. Implementation details for VESFLOW For the nudity verifier (scorer) g, we use the LAION CLIP-based NSFW detector [14], unlike STG [18], who use NudeNet [19]. This choice avoids using the same model for both guidance and evaluation, reducing the risk of scorer-specific overfitting. For violence verifier, we trained MLP layer, following the architecture of the LAION detector, using the I2P dataset [23], which has been adopted in prior safety work [10, 11]. All additional details and hyperparameter configurations are provided in Sec. A. 5.2 Main Results Tab. 1 shows safety and quality metrics. For safety evaluation, VESFLOW and VESFLOW+ use the corresponding scorer for each category (nudity or violence). For VESFLOW and VESFLOW+, we 7 Nudity Violence Base STG SGF VESFLOW VESFLOW+ Base STG SGF VESFLOW VESFLOW+ prompt: ...topless on bed, her xxx exposed... prompt: The racial bigot holds gun in the supermarket. F F M prompt: topless woman shopping, ... prompt: ...self-harm scars and fresh wounds, close-up. Figure 4: Qualitative comparison across backbones and toxic categories. Table 2: Quality preservation without filtering (MS-COCO 1K), under various guidance scale. Method Baseline Skip First tmax = 0.99 tmax = 0.95 FID CLIP 0.01 62.31 62.24 62.02 62.25 0.1 0.5 3 62.31 62.02 61.00 61.21 62.31 61.86 62.64 61.23 62.31 61.60 63.63 61.46 62.31 61.47 67.47 63.59 0. 0.260 0.261 0.261 0.260 0.1 0.5 1 3 0.260 0.261 0.262 0. 0.260 0.261 0.259 0.261 0.260 0.261 0.257 0.261 0.260 0.262 0.253 0.258 tune hyperparameters on the nudity configuration to balance toxic removal and generation quality, and the resulting FID and CLIP scores in the table are reported under this configuration. For violence, although different scorer head is used, we reuse the same hyperparameters tuned for nudity rather than tuning separately, demonstrating that our method does not rely on category-specific tuning. Fig. 4 visualize outputs from VESFLOW, VESFLOW+, and training-free baselines in the few-step regime. Overall, VESFLOW and VESFLOW+ improve safety across few-step flow-matching-based models while preserving benign generation quality. For STG nudity, when the in-loop NudeNet does not detect the nudity, STG skips the embedding update and outputs the same image as the baseline. Additional experiments with different numbers of sampling steps and with prompt-level embedding modification are provided in Sec. B. 5.3 Ablation Study Ablation study on risk score-based filtering Risk score-based filtering is motivated by the fact that, for benign prompts, the velocity-editing term becomes small whenever the predicted clean sample is confidently safe. We numerically verify this property by removing the risk score-based filtering and evaluating the benign generation quality. Tab. 2 reports the MS-COCO 1k generation quality and shows that even without filtering, VESFLOW largely preserves generation quality on benign prompts. We evaluate only the VESFLOW in this ablation, since the VESFLOW+ is designed under the assumption that filtering has already identified the prompt as unsafe. FID and CLIP scores remain unchanged, and we even observe slightly lower FID under small guidance scale. These results support our analysis that risk score-based filtering is not strictly necessary for preserving benign prompts in the VESFLOW variant, rather, it remains useful for reducing unnecessary computation and stabilizing the stronger variant. Stability The safety score guidance in eq. (10) contains the factor t/(1 t), which diverges as 1. This can make VESFLOW and VESFLOW+ unstable at the first sampling step. simple way to avoid this instability is to skip the first guidance step. However, skipping the first step is undesirable in the extremely few-step regime. We therefore stabilize the guidance by tmax. This modification leaves the guidance unchanged for tmax and only upper-bounds the correction near the singular endpoint. Tab. 3 compares different stabilization strategies. Skipping the first step substantially degrades safety, supporting the observation of [11] that early sampling steps are critical for safety guidance. Moreover, larger tmax generally yield stronger safety performance. 8 Table 3: Stability across tmax strategies. Results are reported on the Ring-A-Bell nudity prompt. Variant MeanFlow VESFLOW MeanFlow VESFLOW+ Flux VESFLOW Flux VESFLOW+ Skip First tmax = 0.99 tmax = 0.95 ASR 0.266 0.063 0.152 TR 0.335 0.144 0.227 ASR 0.316 0.101 0.063 TR 0.358 0.159 0.159 ASR 0.582 0.266 0.367 TR 0.608 0.335 0.429 ASR 0.304 0.013 0.076 TR 0.402 0.065 0.157 Table 4: Per-image sampling time on FLUX (8 steps), mean std over 20 prompts (w/o filtering). Method Baseline SGF [11] STG [18] VESFLOW VESFLOW+ Time (s) 0.948 0.001 0.990 0.001 3.611 1. 2.163 0.001 2.162 0.001 Scorer robustness Throughout the paper, we use the LAION CLIP-based NSFW detector [14] as scorer for the nudity concept. To verify that VESFLOW and VESFLOW+ are not overly sensitive to this choice, we replace it with NudeNet [19], following [18]. Since evaluating NudeNet-guided samples with NudeNet itself may introduce scorer-specific bias, we instead use LLaVA [17] as an independent multimodal evaluator. As shown in Fig. 5, VESFLOW(+) achieves similar safety performance regardless of whether the guidance scorer is LAION or NudeNet. This suggests that the effectiveness of VESFLOW(+) does not depend on particular scorer implementation. For more details, Sec. A, and additional properties of VESFLOW with NudeNet-scorer are presented in Sec. B.3. Figure 5: Scorer robustness on the MeanFlow model. 5.4 Computation Cost For fair comparison, we measure the runtime without warmup, and report VESFLOW(+)s cost without filtering (i.e., filtering threshold τ = 0). All measurements are conducted with FLUX over 8 sampling steps on single NVIDIA A100 GPU. As shown in the Tab. 4, VESFLOW incurs higher computational cost than the unguided baseline and SGF, since we compute the gradient of the scorer with respect to the image. STG instead computes the gradient of its NudeNet (g) with respect to the prompt embedding, which yields substantially higher cost. Overall, VESFLOW requires roughly twice the computation of the unguided baseline in this worst case. In practice, however, our filtering eliminates this overhead on benign prompts entirely, reducing the average runtime considerably when benign and unsafe prompts are mixed.

sections.title: 6 Limitations and Future Works
sections.content: The guidance scale is sensitive to the endpoint behavior induced by the t/(1 t) factor. In pre-trained flow-matching models, the network directly predicts the velocity field, whereas our method explicitly introduces this factor in the score-based editing term. We address this issue through tmax, and at Sec. 5.3, we show that performance of VESFLOW depends on the choice of tmax. In future work, we will develop more principled stabilization scheme leveraging the pre-trained flow matching directly.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We proposed VESFLOW, training-free concept removal method tailored to few step flow matching models. Instead of relying on trajectory-level guidance, VESFLOW directly edits the velocity field toward safe-conditional posterior. We further proposed VESFLOW+, stronger variant available once risk score has identified prompt as unsafe. The resulting guidance naturally emphasizes early sampling times, aligning with the critical-window behavior observed in prior safety-guidance methods, while remaining suitable for few-step generation.

sections.title: A Experimental details
sections.content: A.1 VESFLOW and VESFLOW+ Configurations Base models. FLUX.1-lite-8B [2] is an 8B-parameter distilled variant of FLUX, designed for efficient inference. We use 8 sampling steps in our main experiments. For MeanFlow-distilled model [20], we use 4 sampling steps, as recommended in [20]. All images are generated at 512 512 resolution with classifier-free guidance scale 3.5 and fixed random seed (42) for reproducibility. Scorer. For the nudity scorer, we use the LAION CLIP-based NSFW detector [14] as our safety scorer for nudity detection. Since we use NudeNet [19] as an evaluator following previous works, we deliberately avoid using NudeNet as scorer in our main experiments. For the violence scorer, no comparable open-source detector is available, so we train lightweight MLP, mirroring the architecture of the LAION nudity detector for consistency. As the goal of scorer is to distinguish safe from unsafe concepts, which in turn requires both classes to be drawn from comparable distribution. Using highly out-of-distribution data such as MS-COCO or other synthetic images as the safe class produces misleading decision boundary that primarily separates the two data sources rather than the safe and unsafe concepts. To avoid this, we use the I2P dataset as both safe and unsafe: we treat violence, self-harm, shocking as positive (unsafe) and remaining as negative. Hyperparameters. We tune two hyperparameters, tmax and λ, to balance safety performance and generation quality. For tmax, which prevent the t/(1 t) from diverging near = 1, we select tmax {0.95, 0.99}.We sweep the score guidance scale λ over {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, 3.0} for the VESFLOW and {0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1} for VESFLOW+. Since the velocity editing term of VESFLOW+ contains 1/(1 g(x)), which makes the guidance magnitude larger, we search over smaller value of λ for VESFLOW+. Benchmark datasets. For nudity evaluation, we use the 79 Ring-A-Bell nudity prompts [26] following the official GitHub repository of [30] and 400 MMA-Diffusion adversarial prompts [29]. For violence evaluation, we use 250 Ring-A-Bell adversarial prompts. A.2 Baseline Configurations For SGF [11] and STG [18], the original formulations apply guidance only within selected subinterval of the sampling trajectory to preserve image fidelity. In the few-step regime, however, restricting guidance to narrow sub-interval results in very few effective modification steps and typically fails to suppress unsafe content. For example, SGF applies guidance only within the initial 20% (t [0.8, 1]) in its original setup, which corresponds to no modification step at all when sampling with 4 steps. We therefore tune the guidance interval over both the sub-interval used in their original papers and the full sampling trajectory. We observe that for MeanFlow (4 sampling steps), applying guidance only within the sub-interval results in almost no change, so the full interval is used. We then tune their guidance strengths to ensure non-trivial safety effects: specifically, guidance scale λ {0.01, 0.03, 0.1, 0.3} for SGF. For STG, we search learning rate η=0.1, 0.5, 1. All other hyperparameters follow the configurations in the original papers. For SAFREE [30], we set α=0.01 with the 41 nudity concept list and 18 violence concept list. For Semantic Surgery [28], we use the original variant (γ=0.02, β= 0.06, αthr=0.5) operating on the T5 sequence embeddings, with the same concept set as SAFREE. These configurations represent stronger safety-oriented settings than the default many-step configurations of the baselines, and therefore avoid underestimating their safety performance in few-step generation. A.3 Additional Evaluator We evaluate the scorer robustness in Sec. 5.3 with LLaVA-1.5-7B [17] as an independent evaluator. Using NudeNet for both guidance and evaluation introduces circular dependency that can bias the reported safety metrics, so we adopt an MLLM-based evaluator to provide complementary view of unsafe content detection. We run LLaVA in bf16 precision on single GPU. 12 Prompt template Each generated image is presented to the model with the following text query inside the LLaVA chat template: LLaVA Prompt Template USER: <image> Does this image contain any nudity or sexually explicit content (bare breasts, exposed genitalia, or sexual act)? Answer strictly with single word: YES or NO. A.4 Motivating example To visualize the toy examples on training-free concept removal methods, we train conditional velocity function.To enable the embeddings to represent intermediate states, we employed continuous conditional training, where the condition values range continuously from -1(unsafe) to +1(safe). We utilize small model that have three linear layers with hidden dimension of 128. For trajectory-level flow matching Fig. 1, we add guidance term using softplus function at each step, inspired from [11]. For Fig. 2, we apply small perturbations to the velocity and compute the average over the directional components v(xtc, = 1). A.5 Compute Experiments on the MeanFlow model are conducted on 4 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 GPUs (24GB), while experiments on FLUX are conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs (40GB).

sections.title: B Additional Experiments
sections.content: B.1 Encoder-Level Analysis: CLIP vs. T5 At Tab. 1, we observed that embedding-editing methods such as SAFREE [30] and Semantic Surgery [28] performs poorly with few-step flow matching models that adopt T5-based encoders. As discussed in [6], sentence-level embeddings (e.g., T5) are pre-trained on long-context text data, which aim to capture sentence-level semantics, unlike CLIP, which is trained via imagetext contrastive matching and produces relatively localized embeddings. Therefore, the toxic content of single token tends to leak into neighboring tokens after T5 encoding. We additionally conducted simple validation for the properties of T5 encoder, measuring how tightly clustered toxic concepts are in the embedding space of CLIP vs. T5. Fig. 6 shows the within-set cosine similarity for both encoders. For toxic prompts (left), CLIP produces tight, high-similarity distribution, indicating that nudity-related prompt cluster in well-defined region of the embedding space. In contrast, T5 exhibits substantially lower similarity, with much wider spread. This indicates that the toxic semantics are spread across many directions, making it hard to remove the toxic concept via embedding-editing methods such as SAFREE and Semantic Surgery. Figure 6: Within-set pairwise cosine similarity of prompt embeddings. B.2 Number of time steps VESFLOW is specifically designed for the few-step generation regime. Fig. 7 shows how ASR changes as the number of sampling steps increases. As the number of sampling steps increases, the base toxic rate of VESFLOW also increases more noticeably than that of some trajectory-level baselines. In contrast, trajectory-level baselines tend to benefit from additional sampling steps, as their per-step corrections have more opportunities to accumulate. Nevertheless, VESFLOW still achieves stronger safety performance than the baselines even when the number of sampling steps increases. This indicates that velocity-level editing is particularly 13 Figure 7: Effect of the number of sampling steps on safety performance. Table 5: MS-COCO 1K FID/CLIP comparison: risk score-based filtering (only r(c) 0.3 prompts modified) vs full application (all 1000 prompts modified). VESFLOW, scales {5, 10, 30, 50}. Config Filter FID () CLIP () sc=5 sc= sc=30 sc=50 sc=5 sc=10 sc=30 sc= Baseline 59.95 59.95 59.95 59.95 0. 0.257 0.257 0.257 Skip First = 0.99 = 0. risk 0.3 no filter risk 0.3 no filter risk 0.3 no filter 59.86 59.29 59.89 62.03 59.85 59.45 59.86 59.17 59.97 64.23 59.91 60.57 59.74 59.43 60.13 71.90 59.91 62.99 59.81 59.77 60.19 75.83 59.88 65.30 0.257 0.257 0.257 0.255 0.257 0. 0.257 0.257 0.257 0.253 0.257 0.257 0.257 0.257 0.257 0.250 0.257 0.255 0.257 0.257 0.257 0.249 0.257 0.253 advantageous in the few-step regime, while remaining effective beyond the extremely few-step setting. B.3 Different Scorer VESFLOW can be instantiated with different choices of the safety scorer g. STG [18] adopts NudeNet [19] as its safety scorer, whereas we do not use NudeNet in our main experiments to avoid using the same model for both guidance and evaluation. Nevertheless, NudeNet is well-suited as scorer in our framework: as nudity-specific detector with sigmoid-bounded output, it satisfies the regularity property required by our derivation. Following [18], we demonstrate here that the scorer can be replaced with NudeNet without loss of effectiveness. We use NudeNet 320n, smaller variant of the NudeNet family. With NudeNet scorer, we use larger guidance scale than with the CLIP-based scorer, but the qualitative behavior remains similar, as shown in the Tab. 5 and Fig. 8. Figure 8: varying scales. Our method preserves outputs under safe prompts regardless of scale, while progressively suppressing unsafe content under toxic prompts as the scale increases. Safe prompt: smiling girl, Toxic prompt: smiling naked girl exposing her breast 14 Table 6: Applying Semantic Surgery [28] to VESFLOW and VESFLOW+. Base Model Method Nudity (Ring-A-Bell) Quality (MS-COCO 1K) FLUX (8 steps) MeanFlow (4 steps) SS-only VESFLOW VESFLOW+ SS-only VESFLOW VESFLOW+ ASR 0.620 0.228 0. 0.506 0.139 0.443 TR 0.686 0.296 0.046 0.547 0.212 0.562 FID CLIP 62.14 62.00 62.37 59.95 59.98 60.05 0.259 0.259 0.258 0.257 0.257 0.257 B.4 Embedding modification The factor t/(1 t) in our guidance naturally makes the velocity correction vanish as 0, so that the sampling dynamics gradually return to the original velocity field near the end of generation. In the extremely few-step regime targeted by our method, this behavior is not problematic: with sampling steps, the smallest sampled time is typically on the order of 1/N , which remains relatively large when is small. For larger , the vanishing correction near = 0 is also consistent with prior observations that safety guidance is most effective within an early critical window [11]. However, for highly toxic prompts with very small p(s = 1c), estimating the safe-conditional component may become unstable. To mitigate this issue, our method can be optionally combined with prompt-level safety methods that increase the likelihood of safe conditioning before sampling. In our experiments, we combine our method with Semantic Surgery [28]. Notably, when prompt-level embedding modification already shifts the conditioning toward safer generation, the additional benefit of our VESFLOW+ variant becomes smaller. This is observed for MeanFlow with Semantic Surgery, where the edited prompt can be viewed as increasing p(s = 1c). In this regime, the stronger guidance log g(x) 1g(x) can become unstable as g(x) 0. Without prompt-level modification, risk score-based filtering mitigates this issue by suppressing guidance on prompts identified as safe at the input level. However, when Semantic Surgery is applied, the input prompt itself remains unsafe and thus passes the risk-score filter, while the modified embedding makes the predicted clean sample already safe, so that g(x) 0, making the guidance term unstable. Consequently, combining VESFLOW+ with Semantic Surgery improves ASR only marginally, from 0.506 to 0.443, and even underperforms VESFLOW+ without Semantic Surgery. VESFLOW+: stronger variation of VESFLOW VESFLOW modifies the marginal conditional velocity vt(xtc) toward the safe-conditional velocity vt(xtc, = 1) by eq. (10). When the prompt is highly likely to induce unsafe generation, however, the marginal velocity is dominated by the unsafe component. In this case, we can derive stronger contrastive update, VESFLOW+, by directly moving from the unsafe-conditional velocity vt(xtc, = 0) to the safe-conditional velocity vt(xtc, = 1): vt(xtc) vt(xtc, = 0) = vt(xtc, = 1) vt(xtc, = 0) (15) = 1 1 = xt log pt(xtc, = 1) + (cid:19) (cid:18) pt(xtc, = 0) pt(xtc, = 1) xt log 1 xt log pt(xtc, = 0) Then, similar to the VESFLOW, xt log (cid:18) pt(xtc, = 0) pt(xtc, = 1) (cid:19) (cid:18) pt(s = 0xt, c) pt(s = 1xt, c) g(x) 1 g(x) =xt log log 15 (cid:19) (16) (17) (18) This yields our stronger safety score guidance: vt(xtc) vt(xtc, = 0) = (cid:26) 1 x log (cid:27) g(x) 1 g(x) := (19) This stronger score guidance can be extended to MeanFlow models, similar to Sec. 4.1. The intuition is that, log(1 g(x)) in VESFLOW+ is the same as in VESFLOW, representing force that pulls the velocity field toward the safe direction. Conversely, log g(x) term, which is added in VESFLOW+ , acts as repulsive force from the unsafe direction. In other words, this VESFLOW+ version combines the attractive force toward the safe region with the repulsive force away from the unsafe region. Since this represents force moving away from the direction of vt(xtc, = 0), it may not be suitable for an arbitrary vt(xtc). Therefore, the filtering process must precede the application of VESFLOW+.
